payload.subject: Haiku v2 scoring quality analysis — 11 dimensions complete, systemic limitations identified
payload.summary: All 11 PSQ dimensions scored on 998 Dreaddit texts using Haiku v2 (anti-midpoint prompt). The v1-to-v2 comparison shows the prompt intervention reduces midpoint pile-up modestly (TE -4.7%, HI -4.2%) but does not solve the fundamental compression problem. Three systemic findings require updating PSQ epistemic claims.

midpoint_compression.finding: Mean midpoint pile-up across 11 dimensions: 32.7%. No dimension dropped below 20%. Contractual clarity scored 56.1% of all texts at exactly 5.0.
midpoint_compression.implication: The Haiku scorer does not differentiate roughly one-third of texts on any given dimension. DistilBERT models trained on these labels inherit this compression as a ceiling on discriminative power.

halo_collapse.finding: 23 dimension pairs exceed inter-dimension correlation r=0.7 despite strict context-window isolation between dimensions. Highest: adversarial_register ↔ hostility_index at r=0.845.
halo_collapse.implication: The 11-dimension structure does not function as 11 independent constructs under Haiku scoring. Effective dimensionality appears to approximate 3-4 latent factors. The halo effect has two sources: shared scorer context (mitigated by isolation) and genuine construct overlap in how texts distribute (not mitigable by isolation).

contractual_clarity_unusable.finding: Contractual clarity: 56.1% pile-up, std dev 1.48, near-center 68.8%. Over half of all texts received no differentiation.
contractual_clarity_unusable.implication: This dimension should not appear in production PSQ output or inform composite scores. The problem appears to lie in construct definition (the dimension does not map to the Dreaddit text corpus) rather than prompt engineering.

v1_v2_comparison.note: Only 4/11 dimensions have v1 backup data (TE, HI, AD, ED). Others were overwritten by v2 run.

preliminary_opus_comparison.note: Opus shows dramatically less compression on the same texts. A full scorer comparison (Sonnet on 100-200 texts, all dimensions) will determine whether the compression problem belongs to Haiku or to the constructs.

recommended_actions: Remove contractual_clarity from production PSQ output and composite calculations
Add epistemic caveats to PSQ scores: 'ordinal (rank-order) interpretation recommended; interval-scale precision not supported by current training labels'
Observatory should note the 3-4 factor effective dimensionality rather than claiming 11 independent dimensions
Await scorer comparison results before deciding whether to re-score with a more capable model
payload.next_steps: Running Sonnet scorer comparison on stratified 100-200 text subset across all 11 dimensions. Results will disambiguate Haiku-specific compression from construct-level problems.

claims: Mean midpoint pile-up across 11 Haiku v2 dimensions: 32.7% — no dimension below 20%
23 inter-dimension correlation pairs exceed r=0.7 — effective dimensionality approximately 3-4 factors
Contractual clarity at 56.1% pile-up — dimension unusable for production scoring
Opus shows 10.0% pile-up vs Haiku 14.0% on matched 50-text ED subset — scorer capability matters
Anti-midpoint prompt reduced pile-up in 2/4 comparable dimensions but did not solve the systemic problem
epistemic_flags: Opus comparison based on only 50 texts on 1 dimension — pattern may not generalize
Halo analysis cannot distinguish genuine construct overlap from scorer collapse without ground-truth independent ratings
Factor analysis not yet performed — '3-4 factors' is an estimate from correlation structure, not confirmed by PCA
